solution: 
```cpp
class Trie {
private:
    struct TrieNode {
        std::array<TrieNode*, 26> children;
        bool isEndOfWord;

        TrieNode() : isEndOfWord(false) {
            for (auto& child : children) {
                child = nullptr;
            }
        }
    };

    TrieNode* root;

public:
    Trie() {
        root = new TrieNode();
    }

    void insert(string word) {
        TrieNode* node = root;
        for (char ch : word) {
            int index = ch - 'a';
            if (!node->children[index]) {
                node->children[index] = new TrieNode();
            }
            node = node->children[index];
        }
        node->isEndOfWord = true;
    }

    bool search(string word) {
        TrieNode* node = root;
        for (char ch : word) {
            int index = ch - 'a';
            if (!node->children[index]) {
                return false;
            }
            node = node->children[index];
        }
        return node->isEndOfWord;
    }

    bool startsWith(string prefix) {
        TrieNode* node = root;
        for (char ch : prefix) {
            int index = ch - 'a';
            if (!node->children[index]) {
                return false;
            }
            node = node->children[index];
        }
        return true;
    }
};

/**
 * Your Trie object will be instantiated and called as such:
 * Trie* obj = new Trie();
 * obj->insert(word);
 * bool param_2 = obj->search(word);
 * bool param_3 = obj->startsWith(prefix);
 */
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(L) where L is the length of the word or prefix
space complexity: O(N * L) where N is the number of words and L is the average length of the words
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses a Trie data structure to efficiently store and search for words and prefixes. Each node in the Trie represents a character, and the path from the root to a node represents a word or prefix. The `insert`, `search`, and `startsWith` operations all run in linear time relative to the length of the input string, making the Trie highly efficient for these operations. The main strength is the fast lookup and insertion for strings with common prefixes, while the main weakness is the potential for high memory usage if the dataset is large and has many unique prefixes.
